Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA

Delta Air Lines said on March 24, 2026, that it is temporarily suspending special services for members of… The post Delta suspends airport perks for members of US Congress as shutdown strains TSA appeared first on AeroTime.

Delta's suspension of congressional perks signals how operational strain from federal shutdowns can cascade into erosion of airline-government relationships and lobbying influence.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Air Transat Scraps New 12-Hour Longest Route Before A Single Flight Takes Off

The leisure operator was due to take off from Toronto to Ghana's capital on June 27. But the route has been pulled before the first departure...

Air Transat's route cancellation before launch suggests insufficient demand or operational readiness challenges for ultra-long-haul leisure routes from secondary markets.

See Simple Flying →

Business Aviation Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Upgrade To Lie-Flat Business Class On Transatlantic Flights?

Booking business class isn't the only way to access lie-flat transatlantic aviation. Most major transatlantic airlines offer upgrades.

Airlines generate substantial ancillary revenue by selling lie-flat upgrades to premium economy passengers, creating a tiered upsell strategy that captures additional margin without adding inventory.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for satellite execs

A shifting geopolitical landscape is driving huge business opportunities for satcoms but also bringing new supply chain and regulatory challenges, industry executives said March 24 at the Satellite Conference in Washington, D.C. The post Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for s...

Satellite operators must balance rapid commercialization opportunities against emerging supply chain vulnerabilities and evolving export controls in an increasingly fragmented geopolitical environment.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts accountability from post-accident investigation to continuous risk identification, fundamentally changing how airlines prevent incidents before they occur.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Reminders of Where We’ve Been, Where We’re Going

Three Moon rocks are on display during a March 24, 2026, event where NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve the National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. NASA leadership provided updates on mission priorities, including sending t...

NASA's 2026 space policy initiatives signal accelerated timelines for lunar missions and potential commercial space integration, requiring aerospace suppliers to align production capabilities with expanded government space demand.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

No Transponder, No Warning: NTSB Details LaGuardia Alert Failure

NTSB says LaGuardia’s surface warning system was "unable to alert."

LaGuardia's surface warning system lacked integration with non-transponder aircraft, exposing a critical gap in ground-based collision avoidance infrastructure.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Expands Swift 100R Engine STC

Amended STC broadens engine coverage for the unleaded avgas.

The expanded STC certification enables broader aircraft compatibility for Swift 100R, accelerating industry transition toward unleaded fuel alternatives and reducing lead emissions across more piston-aircraft segments.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

Operators must formally document their safety management system alignment with FAA standards to demonstrate compliance and avoid enforcement action.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

3 Ways Students Can Get Involved With Artemis

NASA’s Artemis program will establish a sustainable lunar presence, unlock new scientific discoveries, and develop technologies for spaceflight to Mars and beyond – and students can help shape this new era of space exploration. As America launches this new Golden Age of innovation and exploration...

NASA's Artemis program offers students direct participation in lunar exploration and Mars technology development, creating early-career pathways in advanced aerospace engineering and space systems.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) held a second briefing, on March 24, 2026, that offered further details… The post NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision appeared first on AeroTime.

NTSB's multi-factorial analysis suggests systemic issues beyond single-point failures may have contributed to the accident, signaling need for comprehensive operational and procedural reviews.

General Aviation Mar 24, 2026

NASA Selects Finalists in Student Aircraft Maintenance Competition

Editor’s note: This advisory was updated on March 24, 2026, to update the finalists list. NASA has selected seven student teams as finalists in the 2026 Gateways to Blue Skies Competition, giving them the resources to help address a critical challenge for U.S. aviation: aircraft maintenance. Chal...

NASA's focus on student aircraft maintenance training addresses a severe industry workforce shortage that directly impacts airline operational reliability and safety.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter accident

Pilots systematically overestimate their ability to react to wire strikes, which remain a leading fatal hazard despite being largely preventable through situational awareness and route planning.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

ResilienX Receives FAA Waiver for Remote BVLOS Operations

ResilienX, Inc., a leader in aviation safety and BVLOS drone networks, announced today that it

FAA waiver enables ResilienX to conduct beyond-visual-line-of-sight operations remotely, advancing commercial drone network scalability and reducing operational constraints for enterprise applications.

Drones Mar 24, 2026

When Wireless Networks Falter, Drones Can Provide Backup Connectivity, According to Stevens Researchers

Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology have developed a drone-based system designed to provide temporary

Autonomous drone swarms can restore critical communications infrastructure within minutes during network outages, offering rapid deployment advantages over traditional ground-based restoration methods.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

America’s new 911 drone is always ready

Drones responding to 911 calls are no longer experimental. Across the US, police departments are already using Drone as First Responder (DFR) programs to get eyes on emergencies faster than patrol units. In many cities, drones arrive in under two minutes, well before officers. But even with that ...

Drone-as-first-responder programs are now operational across multiple U.S. police departments, reducing emergency response visualization time to under two minutes and creating new regulatory and operational integration requirements for aviation authorities.

See DroneDJ →

General Aviation Mar 24, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Frederick Municipal Airport, MD (FDK)

Frederick Municipal is a small general and corporate aviation airport located just east of the city of Frederick, Maryland. The traffic mix runs the spectrum from single engine training aircraft up th

Frederick Municipal Airport's diverse traffic profile from trainers to corporate aircraft demonstrates the economic vitality of regional general aviation hubs serving both flight training and business communities.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Air Cargo Mar 24, 2026

How Much Do Cargo Pilots Make Compared To Passenger Airline Pilots?

Passenger airline pilots can earn more, but being a cargo pilot is also a lucrative career.

Cargo pilots command competitive salaries despite fewer career development opportunities, making the niche sector increasingly attractive to pilots seeking stability over traditional airline advancement paths.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA X-Ray Mission Gets Fresh Look at 2,000-Year-Old Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, helping fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have observed. When astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory previously targeted RCW 86, they discovered that a large “c...

IXPE's polarimetry data on ancient supernovae reveals magnetic field structures invisible to traditional X-ray observatories, enhancing understanding of high-energy astrophysical phenomena applicable to space weather prediction.

See NASA →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

SparkPNT Enters the Next Chapter with Facet FP

Designed with the belief that users should have total control over the devices they own,

SparkPNT's partnership with Facet FP prioritizes user ownership and control, signaling a shift toward transparency and independent management in GPS/PNT systems.

See sUAS News →

Industry Mar 24, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across three domains—airlines, airports, and flight operations—offers near-term emissions reductions without waiting for revolutionary new aircraft technologies.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Moog taps Redwire to provide solar arrays for Meteor

WASHINGTON — Redwire announced the first award for its new solar arrays, a $12.8 million contract from Moog. Under the contract announced March 24, Redwire will deliver Extensible Low-Profile Solar Array (ELSA) wings for Moog’s Meteor satellite bus ordered by an undisclosed national security cust...

Redwire's commercial solar array technology is now qualified for national security satellite missions, signaling growing private sector consolidation in defense space capabilities.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: LaGuardia Surface Alert System Did Not Trigger

Investigators said the fire truck involved did not have a transponder, and a stepped-on radio transmission remains under review.

The LaGuardia incident exposes a critical gap: ground vehicles without transponders remain invisible to modern airport collision avoidance systems, highlighting infrastructure modernization needs.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

IAA Prosecutes Drone Operator for Impeding Investigation and Breach of Direction

The Irish Aviation Authority (IAA) brought a prosecution in the District Court against a drone operator for impeding an official investigation

Regulatory enforcement against drone operators now extends to obstruction charges, signaling authorities will prosecute both operational violations and resistance to investigations.

See sUAS News →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Here’s Why Airlines Keep Refurbishing Boeing 777-300ER Cabins Instead Of Replacing Them

Airlines refurbish their Boeing 777-300ER cabins as it offers a more cost-effective solution and improves the efficiency of an aircraft's lifecycle.

Extended aircraft lifecycles through cabin refurbishment defer massive capital expenditures while airlines navigate production delays and supply chain constraints.

FAA Updates GNSS Interference Guide Months After First Release

Update adds pilot procedures and expands detail on recognizing and reporting jamming and spoofing.

The expanded guidance now equips pilots with actionable detection and reporting procedures for GPS threats, moving beyond theoretical awareness to practical operational mitigation.

Sustainable lunar development requires integrated infrastructure systems rather than isolated mission-focused approaches, fundamentally shifting how aerospace companies should plan long-term lunar operations.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

China’s Astronstone raises $29 million for reusable rocket with chopstick-style recovery

Astronstone, one of China’s younger launch startups, has secured new funding as it builds toward the first flight of its reusable AS-1 rocket. The post China’s Astronstone raises $29 million for reusable rocket with chopstick-style recovery appeared first on SpaceNews.

Astronstone's chopstick-style booster capture mechanism represents a novel Chinese alternative to traditional landing legs, reducing reusability complexity and operational costs.

SDA's operational bottleneck has shifted from production to orbital integration and network validation, signaling maturation challenges typical of large constellation deployments.
